coredata.dc:description: Carbon-based nanomaterials, including carbon dots (CDs), are renowned for their photoluminescent properties and broad-ranging applications. CDs were synthesized via hydrothermal treatment of Pereskia aculeata (ora-pro-nóbis) leaves, a sustainable biomass precursor. A Box–Behnken design was employed to systematically investigate the influence of three synthesis parameters: copper ion concentration, reaction temperature, and residence time, on the fluorescence quantum yield (QY). The optimized condition (200 °C, 3 h, without Cu2+) yielded the highest QY of 3.27 %. Statistical analysis revealed that temperature and time had a positive influence on QY, whereas Cu2+ doping resulted in a reduction in fluorescence intensity. The synthesized OPN-derived CDs (OPN-CDs) were spherical, amorphous, and water-soluble, with an average diameter of 3.35 nm ± 1.14 nm. OPN-CDs exhibited consistent qualitative fluorescence behavior over the glyphosate concentration range tested (0.3 μM – 6 μM). To assess their potential applicability, a preliminary glyphosate detection test was performed using OPN-CDs synthesized with and without copper, as well as with Cu2+ added post-synthesis, demonstrating “on–off” or “off–on” characteristics depending on the role of copper. The Cu2+, OPN-CDs (OPN-CDs doped with Cu2+), demonstrated a direct decrease in fluorescence upon glyphosate exposure. These findings highlight the innovative use of Pereskia aculeata as a green, multifunctional precursor for the synthesis of carbon dots and underscore the potential of OPN-CDs in environmentally relevant applications, including water quality monitoring, pollutant detection, and sustainable sensing technologies.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of Pereskia aculeata–Based carbon dots toward qualitative glyphosate detection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon-based nanomaterials, including carbon dots (CDs), are renowned for their photoluminescent properties and broad-ranging applications. CDs were synthesized via hydrothermal treatment of Pereskia aculeata (ora-pro-nóbis) leaves, a sustainable biomass precursor. A Box–Behnken design was employed to systematically investigate the influence of three synthesis parameters: copper ion concentration, reaction temperature, and residence time, on the fluorescence quantum yield (QY). The optimized condition (200 °C, 3 h, without Cu2+) yielded the highest QY of 3.27 %. Statistical analysis revealed that temperature and time had a positive influence on QY, whereas Cu2+ doping resulted in a reduction in fluorescence intensity. The synthesized OPN-derived CDs (OPN-CDs) were spherical, amorphous, and water-soluble, with an average diameter of 3.35 nm ± 1.14 nm. OPN-CDs exhibited consistent qualitative fluorescence behavior over the glyphosate concentration range tested (0.3 μM – 6 μM). To assess their potential applicability, a preliminary glyphosate detection test was performed using OPN-CDs synthesized with and without copper, as well as with Cu2+ added post-synthesis, demonstrating “on–off” or “off–on” characteristics depending on the role of copper. The Cu2+, OPN-CDs (OPN-CDs doped with Cu2+), demonstrated a direct decrease in fluorescence upon glyphosate exposure. These findings highlight the innovative use of Pereskia aculeata as a green, multifunctional precursor for the synthesis of carbon dots and underscore the potential of OPN-CDs in environmentally relevant applications, including water quality monitoring, pollutant detection, and sustainable sensing technologies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are carbon-based nanomaterials with superior fluorescent performance, low toxicity, high biocompatibility, good water dispersibility, and accessible synthesis and functionalization. Since their accidental discovery in 2004 (Xu et al., 2004), these nanomaterials have gained significant attention and have become widely used in various applications, especially in biomedical (Su et al., 2020; Atchudan et al., 2025; Krishnaiah et al., 2025) and environmental fields, such as analyte sensors (Hebbar et al., 2023; Balaji et al., 2025; Atchudan et al., 2024). The surface of CDs is rich in functional groups with nitrogen and oxygen atoms in different structures, and has a hybridized carbon core (Li et al., 2021; Gupta et al., 2021; Sun and Lei, 2017). The use of different carbon precursors and synthesis methods in their production results in different configurations (Liu et al., 2020; Kang and Lee, 2019; Liu et al., 2019; Lin et al., 2021). Notable methods include pyrolysis (Tan et al., 2014; Hu et al., 2019), microwave-assisted synthesis (Venugopalan and Vidya, 2023; Suhail et al., 2020), and the hydrothermal technique (Zhang et al., 2022; Ye et al., 2020; Xie et al., 2019).

Due to the low cost and environmental friendliness, the hydrothermal method has been extensively used for CDs synthesis (Dolai et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2022). Hydrothermal synthesis consists of a thermochemical decomposition of biomass in an aqueous medium using water or another solvent at self-generated pressure (Bressi et al., 2023). The synthesis reaction involves hydrolysis and carbonization of the precursor (Ng et al., 2021), which is directly influenced by the reactivity of the carbon source and consequently affects the nucleation and carbon growth steps (Khairol Anuar et al., 2021). Furthermore, reaction time and temperature are critical parameters that, when varied, change the structure and optical properties of the CDs (Ahuja et al., 2022).

The carbon source is another important variable for CDs production via hydrothermal synthesis. This method favors the use of alternative sources such as carbon precursors, such as biomass, since most biomass contains small organic molecules (Liu et al., 2019). Different biomasses have been used for carbon dots production via hydrothermal methods, e.g., broccoli (Arumugam and Kim, 2018), orange (Senol and Bozkurt, 2020; Li et al., 2021; Bai et al., 2021), banana (Atchudan et al., 2020; De and Karak, 2013; Nguyen et al., 2022; Chaudhary et al., 2020); Coccinia grandis (Karuppasamy et al., 2024; Chandrasekaran et al., 2020), rice husk (Wang et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2015), grass (Praneeth Thota et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2012), bamboo leaves (Liu et al., 2014; Yang et al., 2020); Azadirachta indica leaves (Yadav et al., 2019), and Tridax procumbens leaves (Kota et al., 2024; Muthamil Selvan et al., 2023). Pereskia aculeata, popularly known as “ora-pro-nóbis” (OPN), is an unconventional edible plant that is easy to cultivate and cost-effective. Its main characteristics include non-toxicity and a rich composition of phenolic compounds, carotenoids, and flavonoids (Nogueira Silva et al., 2023). Despite its similar features to other reported biomass precursors, there is no report of CDs production from OPN leaves up to the present moment.

While many studies highlight the use of biomass for CDs production, nanoparticles derived from these materials exhibit low quantum yields (Liu et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2017; Rodríguez-Carballo et al., 2023; Fan et al., 2022). Strategies such as doping and surface functionalization are employed to enhance CDs’ optical properties and multifunctionality (Xu et al., 2017; Miao et al., 2020; Nguyen et al., 2022). Doping alters the structural properties of CDs by inserting heteroatoms into their structure, thereby modifying their fluorescent intensity (Miao et al., 2020). According to the nature of the dopant used, doping can also be categorized into metallic and non-metallic doping by insertion. Due to their heterogeneous nature, biomass are rich in heteroatoms such as N and S, which can functionalize the surface of CDs during the synthesis (Lin et al., 2021; Bressi et al., 2023), in a one-pot process.

Metallic ions have more electrons and unoccupied orbitals; therefore, adding them as dopants during the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) alters their electronic structure (Li et al., 2022; Lin et al., 2018). Among metal dopants, copper stands out due to its low cost, low toxicity, multiple oxidation states, and ability to increase the fluorescence of carbon dots. Copper introduces new energy levels and improves surface passivation, which can lead to increased quantum yield and emission intensity (Guo et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2020).

In this context, the chemical composition of carbon dots (CDs) is intrinsically linked to the choice of precursors, while synthesis parameters play a crucial role in determining their properties during formation. Therefore, adjusting these factors is essential to produce suitable CDs from different precursors (Wahyudi et al., 2023).

Herein, the statistical design of experiments (DoE) approach was employed to systematically explore the effects of reaction temperature, synthesis time, and Cu2+ concentration on the quantum yield of the resulting OPN-derived CDs. To achieve this, a Box-Behnken Design (BBD) was applied. BBD is a response surface methodology that enables efficient modeling of interaction effects with a reduced number of experiments, making it suitable for multivariable optimization in nanomaterial synthesis (Ferreira et al., 2007). This method has been successfully used in previous studies to optimize nanomaterial synthesis conditions (Sun et al., 2022; Da Silva Júnior et al., 2021) providing reliable predictions and enhanced process understanding.

To assess the applicability of the synthesized CDs, the influence of copper presence on the fluorescence response of the OPN-CDs was evaluated in a fluorescence-based proof-of-concept test. In this test, glyphosate was selected as a model analyte for a preliminary fluorescence response test, based on its strong ability to coordinate with Cu2+ ions. The purpose of this test was to evaluate whether the presence of copper, either added externally or incorporated during synthesis, can modulate the fluorescence response of OPN-CDs.

Therefore, by analyzing and evaluating the results of this study, it is possible to understand how unconventional biomass, such as Pereskia aculeata (OPN) leaves, can serve as a viable and sustainable precursor for the synthesis of carbon dots. Here, the importance of optimizing synthesis parameters (reaction temperature, time, and copper doping) using a Box-Behnken Design to maximize the quantum yield of the resulting nanomaterial was highlighted. This work contributes to advancing the scientific understanding of how biomass composition and process conditions affect the structural and optical properties of carbon-based nanomaterials. Furthermore, the application of OPN-CDs in a preliminary glyphosate detection test demonstrates their potential as functional materials in environmental monitoring. Overall, this study reinforces the importance of integrating green chemistry principles into nanomaterial synthesis and supports the development of low-cost, eco-friendly solutions for environmental treatment and monitoring.

Glyphosate (P.A. 98 %; Sigma-Aldrich), quinine sulfate (Dinâmica), sulfuric acid (Synth), and copper (II) nitrate trihydrate (Neon) were used as reactants. Powdered Pereskia aculeata leaves were commercially obtained (Torres Alimentos, Goiânia, Goiás, Brazil). Throughout all experiments, distilled water and analytical-grade reagents were used.

The Infinite M200 plate reader (Tecan) was used to collect the emission, excitation, and absorption UV–Vis spectra from the produced carbon dots and collected from the detection test. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) identified the functional groups present on the CDs’ surface in KBr pellets in the 400 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1 range using an Agilent Technologies – Cary 660 FTIR spectrometer. CDs Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis was performed using the JEM-1011 TEM transmission electron microscope (100 kV). ImageJ software was employed to measure the particle size. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was carried out by using the X’Pert Pro diffractometer (PANalytical) with an X’Celerator detector, using CuKα1,2 radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å).

The Box-Behnken design (BBD) was used to evaluate the effect of combining synthesis parameters of time (h), reaction temperature (°C), and Cu2+ concentration (M), on the quantum yield (QY) of carbon dots synthesized from OPN. The statistical analysis and experimental design were obtained using Statistica® software, version 13.5. The experimental matrix followed a three-level BBD layout and comprised 15 experimental runs, with 12 points positioned at the midpoints of the edges of the experimental cube, where one factor is fixed at the central level while the other two are varied, and three replicates at the central point (0, 0, 0) were included to estimate pure error and assess reproducibility. The factors and corresponding levels are presented in Table 1 . The response variable was the fluorescence quantum yield, expressed as a percentage.

Hydrothermal synthesis was performed using commercial powdered leaves of OPN. Carbon dots were synthesized in a stainless-steel hydrothermal reactor with constant agitation at 500 rpm. In a closed vessel, 1 g of powdered OPN leaves was added to 100 mL of a solution predetermined by the experimental design (distilled water or Cu2+ at 0.5 M or 1 M). The solution was maintained at a constant temperature of 100 °C, 150 °C, and 200 °C for 1 h, 3 h, and 5 h. The reactor was allowed to cool after the reaction, and the resulting suspension was centrifuged for 30 min at 4,000 rpm to separate larger particles. Subsequently, the supernatant was filtered by a 0.22 μm cellulose acetate membrane under vacuum. The CDs’ solution was kept refrigerated and freeze-drying for further characterization. The synthesis scheme used in this work is shown in Fig. 1 .

The CDs quantum yield obtained from the experimental design was determined using a 0.1 M quinine sulfite in sulfuric acid solution as a reference (QY = 0.54). Eq. (1) (Grabolle et al., 2009) was used to determine the carbon dots’ QY:

where A stands for absorbance, I correspond to the integral of the fluorescent emission intensity area, and η is the refractive index of the solvents (ηs/ηst is equal to 1). The subscript “s” denotes the CDs, and “st” denotes the quinine sulfate reference solution. The fluorescence emission spectrum and the absorbance of the quinine sulfate solution and the CDs were measured at 360 nm excitation. Subsequently, the intensity of the fluorescent emission was integrated, and finally, the quantum yield of the CDs was calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of copper on detection performance
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the role of the presence of copper in detection using the OPN CDs synthesized, three experimental conditions were tested to compare the influence of copper when incorporated into CDs during synthesis (doped) or added later in solution. In the first step, detection was tested using 1 mL of the OPN-CDs solution (sample H3), synthesized without copper and exhibiting the highest QY, mixed with the addition of 1 mL of 6 μM glyphosate solution. In the second step, 1 mL of OPN-CDs was first mixed with 1 mL of 1 M copper solution. The mixture was stirred and allowed to stand for 15 min at room temperature, 1 mL of a 6 μM glyphosate was added. In the last scenario, it was tested using 1 mL of the CDs solution synthesized under the same conditions as OPN-CDs but with copper (sample H4 named Cu2+, OPN-CDs), adding 1 mL of a glyphosate solution with a concentration of 6 μM. In all scenarios, the mixture with glyphosate was stirred and allowed to stand for 15 min at room temperature. The fluorescence emission spectrum was measured between 390 nm and 600 nm with excitation at 360 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of synthesis through Box-Behnken experimental design
ce:section.ce:para: Observations from Table 2 indicate that the chosen synthesis parameters significantly impact the obtained quantum yield. The highest quantum yield achieved was 3.27 % in synthesis H3, conducted without copper solution at 200 °C for 3 h. Conversely, the lowest quantum yield, 0.08 %, was obtained in synthesis H4, conducted in the presence of 1 M copper solution, with the same reaction time and temperature as the synthesis with the highest quantum yield. This demonstrates the substantial influence of copper presence on the response variable.

Overall, experiments containing the Cu2+ solution resulted in low quantum yield. For [Cu2+] of 0.5 M, the quantum yields (experiments H9, H10, H11, H12, H13, H14, H15) were intermediate in the range analyzed. Experiments with a 1 M copper concentration at high temperatures (H4) and intermediate temperatures (H6 and H8) led to lower quantum yields.

Experiment H2 exhibited an intermediate QY among the obtained results, conducted in the presence of 1 M Cu2+, at low temperature, and with intermediate reaction time. This moderately satisfactory outcome in the presence of copper might be linked to other synthesis parameters, particularly the intermediate reaction time. The presence of copper ions as dopants for CDs is documented in the literature for analyte detection due to their capacity to enhance fluorescence compared to non-doped material (Mu et al., 2019; Duan et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2022). The affinity between the analyte and the metal, which initially quenched the CDs fluorescence, leads to enhanced fluorescence during detection. Some studies report the utilization of CDs as sensors for metals such as Cu2+, and the presence of these elements is detected through the quenching of CDs fluorescence (Chaudhary et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2014; Huang et al., 2021; Yu et al., 2015; Gedda et al., 2016).

In contrast to studies that reported an increase in CDs fluorescence intensity resulting in higher QY (Guo et al., 2021; Lin et al., 2021), it was observed in the experiments conducted in this work that the presence of Cu2+ as a dopant reduced the QY of the CDs. The reduction in QY can be followed by comparing the experiments conducted under the same time and temperature conditions with and without the presence of Cu2+ (H3 and H4, H5 and H6, and H7 and H8). This behavior may result from the interaction of the metal with the functional groups in the biomass structure used as a precursor, consistent with studies that have already employed CDs for copper detection through fluorescence quenching. Hence, it is likely that the presence of Cu2+ suppressed the CDs' initial fluorescence (Chaudhary et al., 2020; Huang et al., 2021; Gedda et al., 2016).

Experiments conducted without the presence of Cu2+ yielded high quantum yields (H3, H7, and H5). In the absence of Cu2+, the reaction performed at low temperature and intermediate time showed low quantum yield. The reaction temperature has a direct influence on the various chemical bond process breaking, which affects the functional groups present on the CDs’ surface, the molecular state of the produced material, and, consequently, the optical properties (Khairol Anuar et al., 2021; Alaghmandfard et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2020).

In addition, experiments H13, H14, and H15 were conducted under identical synthesis conditions and represent the replicated center points of the Box-Behnken design. These points serve a critical role in evaluating the reproducibility and precision of the synthesis method. The quantum yield values obtained, 0.29 %, 0.17 %, and 0.28 %, resulted in a mean of 0.25 % with a standard deviation of 0.067 %. The variance among these center points (0.00449) was found to be comparable to the model’s residual mean square (MS Residual = 0.00395, see Table 4), confirming that the observed variation is not statistically significant and is consistent with expected experimental variability. The slight differences may be attributed to the natural compositional heterogeneity of the biomass precursor, which contains high levels of carbohydrates (∼78 %), proteins (∼10 %), and fibers (Oliveira et al., 2019). This variety of components in its structure favors the presence of different functional groups to functionalize the CDs’ surface without adding other chemical products. Despite this, the center point reproducibility supports the statistical robustness of the experimental design and validates the model’s predictive capability.

The QY results for these syntheses align with other studies that utilized leaves biomass as a carbon source. CDs produced from grass via hydrothermal treatment for 3 h at 200 °C exhibited a QY of 6.2 % (Liu et al., 2012). Bamboo leaves were used to produce CDs for 6 h at 200 °C, using the same synthesis method, resulting in a QY of 7.1 % (Liu et al., 2014). The same method was employed for CDs production from coriander leaves, 4 h at 240 °C, resulting in a quantum yield of 6.5 % (Sachdev and Gopinath, 2015). In addition, it is reported that the QY obtained from leaves is higher than those obtained using seeds and stems (Omar et al., 2022).

The highest QY obtained in this study was 3.27 % in experiment H3, produced under the following conditions: temperature of 200 °C and a reaction time of 3 h without Cu2+ presence. This is consistent with the values observed for other CDs produced from natural sources via hydrothermal processes, as shown in the Table 3 , with a quantum yield of less than 5 %.

The QY value can be attributed to a combination of structural and surface factors intrinsic to biomass precursors. CDs produced from natural sources usually exhibited excitation-dependent emission, which is often associated with a broad distribution of particle sizes and diverse surface emissive sites (Liu et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2020). Smaller carbon dots tend to transfer energy to larger ones via the quantum confinement effect, resulting in partial fluorescence quenching and diminished QY (Ozyurt et al., 2023). Furthermore, the rich variety of surface functional groups derived from Pereskia aculeata can introduce emissive traps (Liu et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2022; Yan et al., 2019). Additionally, synthesis conditions that promote excessive graphitization, such as elevated temperature and prolonged reaction time, may reduce defect-related emissive centers, further lowering the QY (Liu et al., 2019; Yan et al., 2019). Despite these factors, the obtained CDs maintained good optical properties and showed promising qualitative sensing performance.

Regarding experimental design, the QY was employed as the response variable for the statistical analysis, and the outcomes were displayed in Table 2. Eq. (2) shows the statistical model derived from the study of QY as a function of copper concentration, temperature, and reaction time as

The model exhibited an excellent fit, with an R2 value of 0.9991, indicating a strong agreement between the experimental and predicted responses. This suggests that the model is suitable for reliably predicting QY within the range of parameter values tested. Fig. 2 illustrates the response surface contours showing the interactions among these design parameters. These surfaces were derived based on the optimal performance conditions identified in Experiment H3 ([Cu2+] = 0, t = 3 h, and T = 200 °C).

The surface shown in Fig. 2 a correlate reaction time with temperature. For high temperatures, higher quantum yields are obtained at intermediate reaction times. Fig. 2c shows that there is a high quantum yield at low Cu2+ concentrations coupled with high temperatures. The same behavior can be seen for intermediate reaction times and low [Cu2+] (Fig. 2b).

The obtained curves describe the observed experimental behavior, satisfactorily predicting the changes in QY with alterations in the variable values. These results indicate the Box-Behnken design's reliability for predicting CDs' synthesis behavior. Furthermore, the results align with the literature, where temperature and time are positively associated with QY (Dela Cruz et al., 2019; Abdullah Issa et al., 2020). When temperature and time are insufficient for advancing the carbonization reaction, there is direct interference in the carbon core formation and surface functionalization, leading to a reduced quantum yield. Additionally, when the synthesis takes place over a suitable period, an increase in temperature can result in higher QY (Abdullah Issa et al., 2020).

The ANOVA to determine statistical significance and the model equation fit with 95 % confidence is shown in Table 4 and Fig. 3 . The coefficient of determination (R2) and the adjusted R2 values were 99.91 % and 99.40 %, respectively. The mean squared error (MS Residual) was 0.003953, indicating low unexplained variation by the model. These data demonstrate that the proposed model is suitable for estimating QY values within the intervals studied.

A p-value of < 0.05 and a high F-value confirm the model's significance and the effectiveness of the variables. Analyzing Fig. 3 and Table 4, it can be observed that the variables

The Pareto diagram shows that the most significant positive effects on QY are from the linear temperature (B) and quadratic time (C2) parameters. This result underscores the significant effect of temperature and reaction time on the synthesis of OPN-CDs, as previously reported. Upon analyzing each variable individually, it is apparent that the copper concentration variable (A) has a negative effect on the QY result, leading to a reduction in QY as the Cu2+ concentration increases. The combined effect of [Cu2+] and temperature exhibits the most pronounced negative impact on quantum yield.

Based on the achieved results, sample H3, which was synthesized without the presence of copper at 200 °C for 3 h and exhibited the highest QY among the examined samples, was chosen for the subsequent phases of characterization and evaluation as a sensor.

Pereskia aculeata is well known for its high protein content and contains bioactive compounds such as flavonoids and phenols, which can be used to functionalize OPN-CDs (Nogueira Silva et al., 2023). The H3 carbon dots sample, hereafter referred to as OPN-CDs, showed a yellowish color in visible light and a bluish color in UV light (365 nm), as seen in the image at the top right of Fig. 4 a.

The OPN-CDs showed a UV–Vis absorption spectrum with a strong peak at 280 nm and a tail at 330 nm (Fig. 4a), attributed to the C = C bonds of the π → π* electronic transitions and the C = O bonds of the n → π* transitions, respectively (Sachdev and Gopinath, 2015; Kalanidhi and Nagaraaj, 2021). This behavior is characteristic of CDs derived from biomass (Liu et al., 2019; Sachdev and Gopinath, 2015; Kalanidhi and Nagaraaj, 2021). The emission spectrum in Fig. 4b reveals a maximum value near 450 nm, achieved at an excitation of 360 nm. Fluorescence intensity was assessed at different excitation wavelengths, demonstrating that the synthesized CDs' fluorescence is wavelength dependent.

FTIR spectra of OPN-CDs (blue line, Fig. 5 a), showed at 3403 cm−1 a broad peak indicating the stretching vibrations of hydrogen-bonded OH and NH groups, which are characteristic of phenolic and amine functionalities (Murugan et al., 2019; Sivanandhan et al., 2022). The peak around 2934 cm−1 corresponds to the C–H stretching vibrations in aliphatic chains (Dhanapalan et al., 2025). A notable peak at 1610 cm−1 can be assigned to C=C or C=O stretching, suggesting the presence of aromatic or carboxyl groups (Meghal et al., 2024; Sivanandhan et al., 2022). The signal at 1386 cm−1 is attributed to C-N (Dhanapalan et al., 2025; El-Azazy et al., 2024), indicating the presence of amine or protein-derived structures. A strong absorption at 1047 cm−1 is associated with C–O–C bonds (Meghal et al., 2024), typically from ether linkages, possibly indicating the presence of polysaccharide structures. This analysis indicates that OPN-CDs could be modified with organic molecules from OPN using hydrothermal method. The hydroxyl and amino groups' presence on the surface ensures the CDs’ high-water solubility (Hou et al., 2020; da Silva Júnior, 2021). Similar bands have been observed in other CDs produced from biomass (Arumugam and Kim, 2018; da Silva Júnior, 2021; Sivanandhan et al., 2022).

The diffractogram obtained from the XRD analysis, shown in Fig. 5b, displays a broad peak centered at approximately 2θ ∼ 20°, characteristic of amorphous materials, corresponding to the (002) plane. The functional groups on the CDs’ surfaces, which are rich in O and N, strongly influence the exhibited amorphous structure (Lou et al., 2021). Other studies employing the hydrothermal method to synthesize CDs from biomass under conditions similar to those of OPN-CDs have also obtained a peak near 2θ = 20° (Arumugam and Kim, 2018; Gedda et al., 2016; da Silva Júnior, 2021). Increasing the temperature leads to core growth and reduces the amorphous region, which affects CD optical properties (Khairol Anuar et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2020).

Fig. 6 a shows the TEM analysis image, and Fig. 6b displays the particle size distribution. The resulting OPN-CDs were spherical and well-dispersed with particle sizes ranging from 1.14 nm to 6.95 nm. Based on statistical analysis of 500 CDs nanoparticles, the OPN-CDs have an average size of 3.35 nm ± 1.14 nm. Notably, the particle distribution is moderately centered around 4 nm. CDs produced from leaves of Scallion (Allium fistulosum), Azadirachta indica, and Tamarindus indica exhibited similar particle sizes, 3.5 nm (Zhang et al., 2020); 3.2 nm (Yadav et al., 2019) and 3.4 nm (Bano et al., 2018), respectively.

Although the literature does not consistently evaluate the relationship between CDs size and synthesis parameters such as time and temperature, it can be concluded that sufficient time is required for carbon nuclei to emerge. When the reaction time is insufficient, molecules remain aggregated and exhibit larger sizes (Khairol Anuar et al., 2021). Regarding time, it only affects properties when the reaction temperature is suitable to ensure dehydration and isomerization reactions (Khairol Anuar et al., 2021). This might be related to the effect of quantum confinement of the conjugated graphite core (Khairol Anuar et al., 2021; Lou et al., 2021). The origin of fluorescence is another aspect that requires further clarification (Kang and Lee, 2019; Ding et al., 2020). Some studies suggest that the photoluminescence of CDs initiates in their graphite core. Therefore, the emission wavelength would be size-dependent (Khairol Anuar et al., 2021). Assuming this consideration, the variation in particle size would affect the emission of CDs and, consequently, their application as a sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence Modulation by Cu2+ in glyphosate detection
ce:section.ce:para: Among the different applications of carbon dots (CDs) described in the literature, their use as sensors for identifying emerging contaminants stands out (Guo et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2016). Glyphosate was selected as a model analyte due to its widespread agricultural use and well-established affinity for Cu2+ ions through its carboxylate, amine, and phosphonate groups. The test was designed to observe whether Cu2+ acts as a quenching or recovery agent and to evaluate the intrinsic ability of OPN-CDs to interact with glyphosate. The widespread use of glyphosate, a highly effective herbicidal agent, poses a significant threat to public health and the ecosystem (Hou et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2016).

To evaluate the potential of OPN-derived carbon dots for glyphosate detection, a concentration range of 0.3 µM to 6 µM was initially selected. This range was chosen based on its environmental relevance, as glyphosate concentrations in matrix soil and surface waters frequently fall within the micromolar level and align well with detection ranges reported in related carbon-dot-based sensing systems. As summarized in Table 5 , reported detection ranges for glyphosate using carbon dots typically span from sub-micromolar to tens of micromolar concentrations, depending on the sensor configuration and detection strategy.

Despite testing across this range, no statistically significant variation in fluorescence intensity was observed between the tested concentrations under the experimental conditions (Fig. 7 ). This consistent fluorescence behavior suggests that the synthesized OPN-CDs act as reliable qualitative “on–off” sensors, effectively detecting the presence of glyphosate. Based on these findings, a concentration of 6 µM was selected for subsequent detection experiments, as it produced a consistent fluorescence signal while minimizing the risk of dilution-related inaccuracies at lower levels.

Furthermore, the selected concentration falls within the detection ranges reported for comparable carbon dot–based fluorescence sensors, reinforcing its relevance for the preliminary evaluation of OPN-CDs in glyphosate detection. Although a full calibration curve was not established, the consistent qualitative fluorescence response observed across the tested range highlights the feasibility of using OPN-CDs for initial sensing applications and supports their potential for future development as a selective and low-cost environmental monitoring platform.

This section aimed to evaluate how the presence of Cu2+, either incorporated during synthesis or added post-synthesis, influences the fluorescence response of carbon dots (CDs) synthesized from Pereskia aculeata (OPN) leaves. Three detection strategies were explored to investigate whether copper enhances, suppresses, or is essential in glyphosate recognition: (i) pristine OPN-CDs, (ii) OPN-CDs with Cu2+ added in solution, and (iii) Cu2+, OPN-CDs synthesized hydrothermally with copper.

Fig. 8 displays step 1, where we assessed the fluorescence emission of the CDs solution (OPN-CDs), synthesized without Cu2+ in solution, before and after the addition of glyphosate. The initial CD solution exhibits an intense fluorescent character in the absence of agrochemical, appearing bluish under 365 nm UV light (Fig. 8a-i). After adding glyphosate to the solution, fluorescent intensity decreases (Fig. 8a-ii and Fig. 8b), resulting in a 61 % reduction. This decrease in fluorescence intensity indicates that the herbicide can quench the CDs' fluorescence, suggesting a quenching effect consistent with an “on–off” detection response.

This fluorescence behavior can be attributed to non-radiative interactions between surface functional groups on CDs and the functional moieties of glyphosate. The decrease in intensity could be linked to the interaction between the carboxylic, amine, and phosphate groups in glyphosate and the functional groups on the CDs’ surface. The OPN-CDs FTIR analysis sample revealed bands characteristic of –NH and –OH groups (Fig. 5a, blue line), which are capable of forming hydrogen bonds and electrostatic interactions with glyphosate phosphonate, carboxyl, and amine groups. The hydrogen atoms in these groups can bind to the oxygen atoms of the hydroxyl and phosphonic groups in glyphosate via hydrogen bonds, which inhibit fluorescent activity (Yuan et al., 2017). This quenching is likely mediated by fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET).

FRET is an electrodynamic mechanism that occurs when the donor fluorescent emission spectrum and the acceptor absorption spectrum overlap (Zu et al., 2017), as seen in Fig. 8c. The overlapping of the emission spectra of OPN-CD and the absorption spectra of Gly suggests that CDs and Gly are donor and acceptor pairs in FRET, respectively. Similar behavior was observed for CDs prepared via a hydrothermal route from citric acid and Tris (Tris(hydroxymethyl)methyl aminomethane), where the presence of glyphosate quenched the fluorescence intensity of the CDs (Yuan et al., 2017).

Step 2 evaluated the effect of free Cu2+ ions on the fluorescence behavior of OPN-CDs. A 1 M Cu2+ solution was added to OPN-CDs solution in a 1:1 vol ratio, stirred, and after 15 min the fluorescent intensity was recorded under 365 nm excitation. The test aimed to determine if there was a difference in fluorescent behavior when Cu2+ was used in the hydrothermal synthesis stage or a later surface modification stage. The sample was named OPN-CDs + Cu2+. Fig. 9 b shows that adding copper to the solution reduces the fluorescent emission of the OPN-CDs sample (green line), as seen in the blue curve. This attenuation mechanism may be due to the strong coordination between Cu2+ ions and the –NH and –OH groups on the surface of the CDs, forming a complex between the CDs and Cu2+ (Fu et al., 2023). Thus, the Cu2+ ions are adsorbed onto the surface of the CDs (Fig. 9a-ii). This quenching can be attributed to static or dynamic mechanisms, where Cu2+ coordination perturbs the excited-state energy transfer within the CDs. Mechanistically, photoinduced electron transfer (PET) or the inner filter effect (IFE) may dominate the quenching process as Cu2+ interacts with electron-rich donor groups on the CDs surface (Zu et al., 2017).

Subsequent addition of glyphosate (6 uM, 1:1 ratio) to the OPN-CD-Cu2+ complex solution led to a slight recovery (partial recovery) in the fluorescent intensity of the CDs (Fig. 9a-iii and Fig. 9b – purple), consistent with an “off–partially on” response. This behavior is attributed to competitive coordination, in which glyphosate's carboxylic, amine, and phosphate groups give it a strong binding capacity to Cu2+ (Fu et al., 2023; Sheals et al., 2001; Wang et al., 2020), making it a chelating agent that can form stable complexes with metal ions (Caetano et al., 2012). This complex is preferentially formed because Cu2+ is weakly adsorbed to the surface of the CDs. As a result, the functional groups present on the surface of the nanomaterial are released, which explains the recovery of part of its initial fluorescence. The available evidence suggests that the interaction between copper ions and glyphosate is stronger than between copper ions and CDs (Fu et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2022; Cao et al., 2024).

In step 3, the fluorescence emission of the CDs synthesized in the presence of Cu2+ [1 M] (sample H4, named Cu2+, OPN-CDs) was evaluated with and without glyphosate. The fluorescent intensity of Cu2+, OPN-CDs solution without glyphosate, was significantly lower, around 85 times, than that obtained for the undoped OPN-CDs (Fig. 10 a). The hydrothermal adhesion of Cu2+ to the CDs significantly reduces their fluorescent intensity compared to CDs synthesized without Cu2+ under the same conditions. This pronounced quenching is attributed to the hydrothermal incorporation of Cu2+, which likely forms stable coordination complexes with surface functional groups on the CDs, leading to disruption of radiative pathways. FTIR analysis (Fig. 5a, red line) supports this hypothesis, revealing new peaks at 2930 cm−1 and 825 cm−1, indicative of –CH stretching vibrations, not present in the undoped sample. The emergence of these new groups suggests the formation of a new complex between Cu2+ and the functional groups on the surface of the CDs. The reduction in the characteristic band of the –NH/–OH bonds at 3403 cm−1 indicates the complexation of these functional groups with Cu2+ (Wu et al., 2022). Additionally, the characteristic bands of the C=C and C=O groups disappear, and the signal of the C-N group at 1386 cm−1 intensifies in the Cu2+, OPN-CDs sample, indicating a bonding rearrangement. The copper ions may be bound by stronger bonds than those reported in scenario 2 due to the processes involved during the synthesis stage (Fig. 10a-i). In this case, fluorescence quenching may be a combination of static and dynamic effects (Wu et al., 2022).

When Glyphosate was added to the Cu2+, OPN-CDs solution (6 μM, 1:1 v/v), the fluorescent intensity was attenuated again (see Fig. 10a-ii and Fig. 10c), indicating an “on–off” sensing mechanism. This behavior contrasts with the partial recovery observed in the Cu2+-post-modified system (Step 2). Here, glyphosate can bind to the residual free functional groups on the surface and the Cu2+ atom attached to the CDs. As previously reported, glyphosate can easily coordinate with Cu2+ from the carboxylic, amine, and phosphate groups present in the agrochemical (Fu et al., 2023; Sheals et al., 2001; Wang et al., 2020).

Sensors that utilize CDs for detection can be classified based on the response to the analyte. In the evaluated steps, detection was performed in different ways. In step 1, the fluorescence of the sensor is altered due to the reaction between the functional groups on the surface of the CDs and glyphosate, showing an “on–off” behavior. In step 2, an “off–on” response appears, where the fluorescent intensity previously suppressed by a fluorophore (Cu2+) is restored after gly addition. In step 3, detection occurs because the sensor is functionalized with a high-affinity receptor, specifically Cu2+ with glyphosate, demonstrating an “on–off” profile (Zhang et al., 2022).

Notably, this “on–off” fluorescence response using Cu2+-doped OPN-CDs distinguishes this work from most existing reports in the literature. In previously described systems, Cu2+ is typically introduced post-synthetically and acts as an external quencher, with glyphosate restoring the fluorescence, resulting in an “off–on” mechanism (Wang et al., 2016; Fu et al., 2023; Wu et al., 2022). In contrast, the Cu2+-doped CDs synthesized in this study demonstrated a direct decrease in fluorescence upon glyphosate exposure. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report of glyphosate detection using copper-doped carbon dots exhibiting an “on–off” fluorescence mechanism. This finding underscores the critical influence of metal ion incorporation mode, during versus after synthesis, on sensing mechanism of carbon dot-based sensors.

By comparison, Fe3+-based carbon dot sensors have also demonstrated “on–off” quenching profiles, but these systems are more vulnerable to interference due to nonspecific interactions with a range of trivalent ions in complex sample matrices (Hou et al., 2020). Zn2+-based platforms, although effective in electrochemical and dual-mode detection contexts, typically exhibit lower fluorescence responsiveness and binding specificity to glyphosate, limiting their application in straightforward photoluminescent assays (Liu et al., 2023).

The distinct fluorescence behaviors observed in our system, ranging from “on–off” quenching in undoped and Cu2+-doped OPN-CDs to partial “off–on” recovery in the presence of free Cu2+, are consistent with mechanisms reported in earlier studies. Prior research has shown that Cu2+ can effectively modulate carbon dot fluorescence through strong coordination with glyphosate, enabling sensitive detection via quenching-recovery pathways. For example, Fu et al. (2022) used BTSC-functionalized CDs quenched by Cu2+, with fluorescence restored upon glyphosate binding, demonstrating a clear “off–on” response (Fu et al., 2023). Wu et al. (2022) reported similar recovery behavior in Cu2+-quenched green-emissive CDs (Wu et al., 2022), and Wang et al. (2023) developed a dual-mode system combining Cu2+ nanoclusters with CDs for glyphosate detection via ratiometric and colorimetric signals (Wang et al., 2024).

While glyphosate was successfully detected across all tested strategies, the use of Cu2+ in Steps 2 and 3 introduces critical considerations related to sensitivity and selectivity. The strong coordination between Cu2+ and glyphosate could limit the availability of the ions for sensing, reducing overall detection efficiency. Additionally, copper ions may interact with other components present in complex media, leading to nonspecific signals or interference (Zhang et al., 2022). In contrast, using OPN-CDs without added metal ions demonstrated reliable detection performance while avoiding these complications. This metal-free approach not only simplifies the sensing system and enhances environmental compatibility, reinforcing the potential of undoped CDs as effective and sustainable qualitative sensors.

The findings of this study showed the potential regarding the utilization of CDs obtained from Pereskia aculeata leaves as sensor devices to qualitatively detect glyphosate, between 0.3 μM and 6 μM, in aqueous environments. The significance of these findings propels the exploration of new research directions.

In summary, based on the results presented in this study, it is possible to conclude that Pereskia aculeata leaves could be employed as carbon precursors for carbon dot synthesis. The findings indicate that, for this synthesis, the presence of Cu2+ as dopants in the nanomaterial negatively affects the quantum yield. On the other hand, the fluorescence quantum yield was significantly positively affected by the synthesis reaction time and temperature. Their stable qualitative fluorescence response within the glyphosate concentration range of 0.3 µM to 6 µM confirmed their potential as “on–off” fluorescent sensors. Notably, the fluorescence quenching behavior was strongly influenced by the form in which Cu2+ was present: OPN-CDs without copper showed direct quenching by glyphosate, with an intensity reduction of 61 %, while Cu2+ added post-synthesis led to a fluorescence recovery effect upon glyphosate addition (“off–on”). In contrast, Cu2+-doped OPN-CDs exhibited an “on–off” fluorescence response, a novel finding, as most reported CD-based glyphosate sensors follow the reverse (“off–on”) mechanism. This distinction highlights the critical role of metal incorporation strategy in modulating sensor behavior. Overall, the synthesized OPN-CDs demonstrate strong potential for application in environmentally relevant fields, particularly in qualitative detection of glyphosate in water. The findings of this study have significant implications for the field of carbon dot synthesis and application, offering a promising avenue for detecting environmental contaminants.
